scope_note: Harvard's official guidance is school-wide rather than major-specific. The admissions office explicitly says there is no one-size-fits-all curriculum formula, so these cards summarize the published baseline preparation advice for all applicants.

areas.name: Overall rigor
areas.summary: Challenge yourself with the strongest curriculum that fits your school context and interests.
areas.detail: Harvard says there is no “one size fits all” rule about secondary-school curriculum, but applicants should challenge themselves with courses deemed appropriate by their teachers and counselors.

areas.name: Advanced-course balance
areas.summary: More AP/IB courses are not automatically better if they create burnout.
areas.detail: Harvard notes that some students thrive with many AP, IB, or other advanced classes, while others benefit from a more balanced schedule that leaves room for extracurricular and personal development.

areas.name: Math
areas.summary: Harvard recommends four years of math; calculus is not required.
areas.detail: Harvard recommends four years of math focused on conceptual understanding, higher-order thinking, and mathematical reasoning. It explicitly says calculus is not a requirement for admission, though students aiming for engineering, computer science, physics, mathematics, statistics, and similar fields may benefit from taking it if available.

areas.name: Foundational depth
areas.summary: Master the fundamentals instead of rushing into advanced classes without the foundation.
areas.detail: Harvard says students can still enter calculus-heavy fields from introductory college calculus courses with no high-school-calculus prerequisite and encourages students to master foundational mathematical material rather than rushing through advanced coursework.

international_note: Harvard says applicants do not all have the same opportunities and course offerings, so rigor is evaluated in the context of what was available at the student's school.
